question: Your tee shot lands in a yellow penalty area (water hazard). What are your options?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1d** - "If a ball is in a penalty area, the player may take penalty relief by adding one penalty stroke and: (1) playing the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made, or (2) dropping the original ball or another ball in the relief area based on a reference line going straight back from the hole through the point where the original ball last crossed the edge of the penalty area." **Ruling**: You have two main relief options, each with one penalty stroke. **Penalties/Procedures**: Add one penalty stroke and either: (1) Play stroke-and-distance relief from where you last played, or (2) Take back-on-the-line relief by dropping behind the penalty area keeping the entry point between you and the hole. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-17.html

question: You hit your tee shot and it goes out of bounds. What must you do?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.2a** - "If a ball is lost or out of bounds, the player must take stroke-and-distance relief by adding one penalty stroke and playing the original ball or another ball from where the previous stroke was made." **Ruling**: You must take stroke-and-distance relief when your ball goes out of bounds. **Penalties/Procedures**: Add one penalty stroke to your score and play your next shot from where you played the previous stroke (the tee for a tee shot). You cannot drop near where the ball went out of bounds. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-18.html

question: Your ball is in a greenside bunker. Can you remove a small stone lying next to your ball before playing your shot?
ground_truth: **Rule 12.2a** - "Before making a stroke at a ball in a bunker, a player may remove loose impediments under Rule 15.1 and movable obstructions under Rule 15.2." **Ruling**: Yes, stones are loose impediments and can be removed from bunkers without penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: You may remove the stone by hand or club without penalty. Be careful not to improve conditions affecting your stroke when removing loose impediments close to your ball. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-12.html

question: Your ball comes to rest on a paved cart path in the fairway. Are you entitled to free relief?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1b** - "If a player's ball lies on or touches an immovable obstruction, or when such an obstruction interferes with the player's stance or area of intended swing, the player may take free relief." **Ruling**: Yes, you are entitled to free relief from the paved cart path. **Penalties/Procedures**: Find the nearest point of complete relief (where the cart path doesn't interfere with ball, stance, or swing), then drop within one club-length of that point, not nearer the hole. No penalty stroke. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-16.html

question: Your ball is on the putting green. Can you repair spike marks in your line of putt?
ground_truth: **Rule 13.1c(2)** - "The player may repair damage on the putting green without penalty by taking reasonable actions to restore the putting green as nearly as possible to its original condition, but only by using his or her hand, foot or other part of the body or a ball-marker, tee, club or similar item of normal equipment." **Ruling**: Yes, spike marks can be repaired on the putting green. **Penalties/Procedures**: You may repair spike marks using your hand, foot, club, or normal equipment. There is no penalty for repairing this type of damage on the putting green. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-13.html

question: In match play, you and your opponent both make a 5 on a hole. What is the result of the hole?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2a(2)** - "If the player and opponent have the same score on a hole, the hole is halved." **Ruling**: The hole is halved (tied) when both players score the same number of strokes. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. The hole result is recorded as halved, and play continues to the next hole. If the match ends with a halved final hole, the match continues in sudden-death playoff until someone wins a hole outright. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-3.html

question: You hit your ball into thick rough and cannot find it. How long do you have to search before it is considered lost?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.2a(1)** - "A ball is lost if not found in three minutes after the player or his or her caddie begins to search for it." **Ruling**: You have three minutes to search before a ball is considered lost. **Penalties/Procedures**: The search time starts when you or your caddie begin looking for the ball. After three minutes, if not found, the ball is lost and you must take stroke-and-distance relief (add one penalty stroke and play from where the previous stroke was made). **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules/rules-2019/rules-of-golf/rule-18.html

question: In match play, your putt from the green hits the flagstick that was left in the hole. Is there a penalty?
ground_truth: **Rule 13.2a** - "There is no penalty if a ball played from the putting green hits the flagstick" **Ruling**: No penalty when your putt hits the flagstick that was left in the hole in match play. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. The ball must be played as it lies from wherever it comes to rest after hitting the flagstick. The player must decide before making the stroke whether to leave the flagstick in the hole or have it removed. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-13

question: In match play, your opponent concedes your next putt. Can you refuse the concession and putt anyway?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2b** - "A concession is final and cannot be declined or withdrawn" **Ruling**: No, you cannot refuse the concession and putt anyway. **Penalties/Procedures**: The concession is binding once made and cannot be declined. You must accept the concession and count the stroke as holed. Your opponent cannot withdraw the concession either. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-3

question: You think your tee shot might be lost in the woods. You announce you will play a provisional ball and hit another. Your original is then found in the fairway. Which ball do you play?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.3c** - "When Original Ball Is Found on Course Outside Penalty Area Before the End of the Three Minute Search Time. The player must play the original ball as it lies" **Ruling**: You must play the original ball that was found in the fairway. **Penalties/Procedures**: Abandon the provisional ball immediately. You cannot make any more strokes with the provisional ball (it becomes a wrong ball). All strokes made with the provisional ball do not count. Play the original ball as it lies with no penalty. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18

question: You take a free drop from an immovable obstruction. You drop the ball from knee height and it bounces and rolls outside the one-club-length relief area. What must you do?
ground_truth: **Rule 14.3c(2)** - "The player must drop a ball in the right way a second time" **Ruling**: You must drop a second time from knee height in the relief area. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. If the second drop also rolls outside the relief area, you must place the ball where it first touched the ground on the second drop. If the placed ball won't stay, place it again on the same spot, and if it still won't stay, place it on the nearest spot where it will stay at rest (not nearer the hole, same area of course). **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-14

question: Your ball is unplayable in a bunker. You decide to take back-on-the-line relief. Must you drop the ball inside the bunker or can you drop outside it?
ground_truth: **Rule 19.3b** - "For a total of two penalty strokes, the player may take back-on-the-line relief outside the bunker" **Ruling**: You have two options for back-on-the-line relief from an unplayable ball in a bunker. **Penalties/Procedures**: (1) For one penalty stroke, you must drop in the bunker using back-on-the-line relief. (2) For two penalty strokes total, you may drop outside the bunker using back-on-the-line relief. The reference line goes straight back from the hole through the original ball spot. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-19

question: Your ball lies in the general area just outside a GUR boundary, but your stance would be inside the GUR. Are you entitled to free relief?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1a(1)** - "An abnormal course condition physically interferes with the player's area of intended stance or area of intended swing" **Ruling**: Yes, you are entitled to free relief because GUR interferes with your stance area. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. Find the nearest point of complete relief where you have no interference from the GUR for your stance or swing. Drop within one club-length of that point, not nearer the hole, in the same area of the course. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-16

question: While searching for your ball in the rough, you accidentally kick it. What is the penalty and what must you do?
ground_truth: **Rule 9.4b Exception 2** - "There is no penalty when the player accidentally causes the ball to move while trying to find or identify it" **Ruling**: No penalty for accidentally moving your ball while searching for it. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. Replace the ball on its original spot (estimate the spot if you don't know exactly where it was). This exception only applies while actually trying to find or identify the ball, not while walking to the search area. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-9

question: In match play, you play out of turn from the tee. Can your opponent make you replay the shot?
ground_truth: **Rule 6.4a(2)** - "There is no penalty but the opponent may cancel the stroke" **Ruling**: Yes, your opponent can make you replay the shot if you play out of turn from the tee. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty, but your opponent may cancel your stroke if done promptly and before either player makes another stroke. If cancelled, you must replay from the tee when it becomes your turn. However, if your opponent agreed to let you play out of turn to save time, they forfeit the right to cancel. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-6

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You find your ball sitting on dry land with a playable lie. Are you required to take a penalty drop, or can you play the ball as it lies?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1b** - "The player may either: Play the ball as it lies without penalty, under the same Rules that apply to a ball in the general area... or Play a ball from outside the penalty area by taking penalty relief" **Ruling**: You can play the ball as it lies without penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty if you play it as it lies. Alternatively, you may take penalty relief outside the penalty area (stroke-and-distance, back-on-the-line, or lateral relief) for one penalty stroke if you prefer. The choice is yours. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-17

question: A movable obstruction (a rake) is lying in a bunker. Your ball is not in the bunker but the rake is on your line of play. Can you move the rake?
ground_truth: **Rule 15.2a** - "Without penalty, a player may remove a movable obstruction anywhere on or off the course and may do so in any way" **Ruling**: Yes, you can move the rake without penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for removing movable obstructions anywhere on the course. If your ball moves while removing the obstruction, replace it on the original spot. Cannot remove movable obstructions to affect a ball already in motion. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-15

question: You start your round with 14 clubs. On the 5th hole you discover that one of the clubs belongs to another player and was placed in your bag by mistake. How many clubs do you have for the purpose of the 14-club limit?
ground_truth: **Rule 4.1b(1)** - "After a player's round has started, if the player picks up another player's club that was left behind, or a club is mistakenly put in the player's bag without their knowledge, the club is not treated as one of the player's clubs for purposes of the 14-club limit" **Ruling**: The mistaken club does not count toward your 14-club limit. You still have only 13 clubs for rules purposes. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty as long as you don't use the other player's club. You must not play any strokes with that club but it doesn't affect your club count. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-4

question: In match play, you realize on the 4th tee that you have been playing with 15 clubs since the start of the round. What is the penalty?
ground_truth: **Rule 4.1b** - "The match score is revised by deducting one hole for each hole where a breach happened, with a maximum deduction of two holes in the round" **Ruling**: Maximum two-hole adjustment penalty applied to the match score. **Penalties/Procedures**: This is a match adjustment penalty, not loss of hole. Since you had the breach for 3+ holes, the maximum two-hole penalty applies. The adjustment is made at the end of the hole being played. You must immediately take action to clearly indicate which club you're taking out of play. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-4

question: Your ball is embedded in the face of a bunker, above the sand, in a grassy area that is part of the bunker wall. Are you entitled to free relief for an embedded ball?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.3** - "Relief is allowed under Rule 16.3b only when a player's ball is embedded in the general area" **Ruling**: No, you are not entitled to free relief. There is no embedded ball relief when the ball is embedded in a bunker face or wall. **Penalties/Procedures**: You must either play the ball as it lies or declare it unplayable under Rule 19 (one penalty stroke). USGA clarifications specifically state there is no free relief for balls embedded in stacked turf or soil faces above bunkers. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules-hub/clarifications-of-the-rules-of-golf.html

question: You play a provisional ball from the tee. Your provisional lands in a red penalty area. Before searching for your original, you play a second provisional from the tee. Is the second provisional valid?
ground_truth: **Rule 18.3a** - "If a provisional ball itself might be lost outside a penalty area or be out of bounds: The player may play another provisional ball" **Ruling**: Yes, the second provisional is valid. Since the first provisional might be lost outside the penalty area or elsewhere on the course, you may play a second provisional. **Penalties/Procedures**: The second provisional has the same relationship to the first provisional as the first has to the original ball. It becomes the ball in play if the first provisional is lost outside the penalty area or out of bounds. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-18

question: Your ball is at rest on the putting green. A strong gust of wind moves it into a bunker. Must you play it from the bunker or can you replace it on the green?
ground_truth: **Rule 9.3** - "If natural forces cause a ball at rest to move, there is no penalty and the ball must be played from its new location" **Ruling**: You must play the ball from the bunker where it came to rest. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. The exception for replacing balls moved by natural forces on the putting green only applies if the ball had previously been lifted and replaced under a Rule. Since the ball was simply at rest and not previously lifted, it must be played from where wind moved it. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/players-rule-finder/players-rule-finder/putting-green/ball-or-ball-marker-moved-by-wind-water-or-other-natural-forces

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. You decide to take lateral relief, but the nearest point within two club-lengths is on the other side of the penalty area. Can you drop there?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1d(3)** - "May be in any area of the course except the same penalty area" **Ruling**: Yes, you can drop on the other side of the penalty area if it's within two club-lengths and not nearer the hole. **Penalties/Procedures**: One penalty stroke. The relief area is measured from where your ball last crossed into the penalty area. The ball can land in any area of the course within two club-lengths except the same penalty area. However, unless there's a local rule allowing opposite-side relief, the ball must come to rest in the same area of course it first touches when dropped. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-17

question: In match play, you and your opponent both play wrong balls on a par 3. You each realize the mistake before teeing off on the next hole. What is the ruling?
ground_truth: **Rule 6.3c(1)** - "If the player and opponent play each other's ball during the play of a hole, the first to make a stroke at a wrong ball gets the general penalty (loss of hole). But if it is not known which wrong ball was played first, there is no penalty and the hole must be played out with the balls exchanged" **Ruling**: If you cannot determine who played the wrong ball first, there is no penalty and the hole stands as played with exchanged balls. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty if sequence cannot be determined. The hole result stands with the exchanged balls. Only if it's clear who played wrong first does that player lose the hole. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-6

question: Your ball is in the general area and an immovable obstruction (a sprinkler head) interferes with your stance. However, the obstruction is inside a red penalty area while your ball is outside it. Can you take free relief from the obstruction?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1a(2)** - "Relief from interference by an abnormal course condition is allowed under Rule 16.1 only when both: The abnormal course condition is on the course (not out of bounds), and The ball is anywhere on the course, except in a penalty area" **Ruling**: No, you cannot take free relief. Since the immovable obstruction is inside the penalty area, no free relief is available even though your ball is outside it. **Penalties/Procedures**: You must play the ball as it lies or take unplayable ball relief under Rule 19 with a penalty stroke. The restriction applies because abnormal course conditions (including immovable obstructions) in penalty areas don't provide free relief. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-16

question: Your ball is plugged in the steep face of a bunker. You declare it unplayable and choose lateral relief (two club-lengths). All points within two club-lengths that are not nearer the hole are outside the bunker in the general area. Where must you drop?
ground_truth: **Rule 19.3a** - "The ball must be dropped in and come to rest in the bunker if the player takes either back-on-the-line relief or lateral relief" **Ruling**: You cannot use lateral relief since all valid drop areas are outside the bunker. **Penalties/Procedures**: Since lateral relief for unplayable balls in bunkers must remain in the bunker, and no such areas exist here, you must use either stroke-and-distance relief (one penalty stroke) or back-on-the-line relief outside the bunker (two penalty strokes under Rule 19.3b). Lateral relief option is not available. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-19

question: In stroke play, you putt from on the green and your ball is still moving when it hits another player's ball that was at rest on the green. What happens?
ground_truth: **Rule 11.1a** - "If the player's ball in motion hits another ball at rest on the putting green and both balls were on the putting green before the stroke, the player gets the general penalty (two penalty strokes)" **Ruling**: You incur a two-stroke penalty. **Penalties/Procedures**: Two-stroke penalty for you. Play your ball as it lies from where it comes to rest. The other player's ball must be replaced on its original spot. This is the only ball-hitting-ball scenario that carries a penalty in stroke play. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/players-rule-finder/players-rule-finder/putting-green/ball-in-motion-deflected-two-balls-played-from-putting-green-collide

question: In match play, you are 3 down with 3 holes to play. On the 16th tee, your opponent concedes the hole. On the 17th, you win the hole. You are now 1 down with 1 to play. On the 18th green, your opponent concedes your birdie putt, tying the match. Your opponent then says they want to withdraw the concession on hole 16 because they made it by mistake. Can they?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2b** - "A concession is final and cannot be declined or withdrawn" **Ruling**: No, your opponent cannot withdraw the concession. **Penalties/Procedures**: Concessions are final regardless of whether they were made by mistake. The concession of hole 16 stands, and the match remains tied. Your opponent cannot withdraw any concession once it has been clearly communicated, even if made in error. The match would proceed to playoff holes or be decided based on the competition's playoff procedures. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-3

question: Your ball is in the general area and you have interference from an animal hole. The nearest point of complete relief would be on a putting green (not the green of the hole being played). Where do you take relief?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1b** - "The nearest point of complete relief must be in the general area" **Ruling**: You cannot take relief on the wrong putting green. You must find the nearest point of complete relief in the general area. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for free relief from abnormal course conditions. The nearest point of complete relief must be in the same area of course (general area) and cannot be on a different putting green. Find the next nearest point in the general area that gives complete relief, drop within one club-length of that point. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules-hub/clarifications-of-the-rules-of-golf.html

question: In match play, you hit your approach shot and it lands on the green. Your opponent, who hasn't played yet, picks up your ball thinking it's theirs. They realize the mistake and replace it. What is the penalty?
ground_truth: **Rule 9.5b Exception 2** - "There is no penalty when the opponent marks the spot of the player's ball and lifts it on the putting green in the mistaken belief that it is the opponent's own ball" **Ruling**: No penalty for your opponent. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. This specific exception covers accidentally lifting the wrong ball on the putting green in match play when done by mistake thinking it's your own ball. Your ball must be replaced on its original spot. The key is that it was done by mistake and on the putting green. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-9

question: Your ball is in a red penalty area. Before playing your next stroke, you ground your club in the penalty area and take a practice swing that touches the ground. What is the penalty?
ground_truth: **Rule 17.1b** - "The player may either: Play the ball as it lies without penalty, under the same Rules that apply to a ball in the general area" **Ruling**: No penalty. Under the 2019 Rules modernization, there is no penalty for grounding your club or touching the ground in a penalty area. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty for grounding club, taking practice swings that touch ground, or moving loose impediments in penalty areas. These actions are now allowed since penalty areas are treated the same as the general area for ground contact purposes. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules-hub/rules-modernization/major-changes/touching-or-moving-loose-impediments-or-ground.html

question: You take relief from a cart path. You determine the nearest point of complete relief and drop within one club-length. The ball comes to rest in a position where you now have interference from a sprinkler head. Must you play it as it lies, or can you take additional relief from the sprinkler head?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1b** - "If there is interference by an abnormal course condition on the course, the player may take free relief" **Ruling**: You can take additional free relief from the sprinkler head. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty. Since you have interference from a different immovable obstruction (sprinkler head) than the one you originally took relief from (cart path), you are entitled to another free relief procedure. Find the nearest point of complete relief from the sprinkler head and drop within one club-length. Each obstruction is treated separately. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/RulesFAQ/rules_answer2019.asp?FAQidx=283&Rule=0&Topic=6

question: In match play, on the final hole, both players are on the green. Player A is 20 feet away, Player B is 6 feet away. Player A putts first and misses, leaving a 2-foot putt. Player B then concedes Player A's next putt. Player A picks up their ball. Player B then three-putts from 6 feet. Player A claims they win the hole because Player B made 5 and their conceded putt means they made 4. Player B says the hole was halved because the concession happened before B finished. Who is correct?
ground_truth: **Rule 3.2b** - "A player may concede any time before the hole is completed" and "has then completed the hole with a score that includes that conceded stroke" **Ruling**: Player A is correct and wins the hole. **Penalties/Procedures**: When Player B conceded Player A's putt, Player A immediately completed the hole with that conceded stroke counting toward their score. Player B's subsequent three-putts result in a higher score than Player A's conceded score, so Player A wins the hole. The concession takes effect immediately when made, regardless of whether the conceding player has finished. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-3

question: Your ball is in a bunker. Before your stroke, you smooth footprints in the bunker that are 10 feet away from your ball and not on your line of play. You do this to care for the course. Is there a penalty?
ground_truth: **Rule 12.2b** - "Touching sand Smoothing the bunker to care for the course" does not result in penalty, "but the player gets the general penalty if their actions in touching the sand improve the conditions affecting the stroke in breach of Rule 8.1a" **Ruling**: Potentially yes, there is a penalty if it improves conditions affecting your stroke. **Penalties/Procedures**: Two-stroke penalty (general penalty) if the smoothing improves conditions affecting your stroke, even if done for course care and away from your ball. The key test is whether your action improves playing conditions, not the intent or location. If no improvement to playing conditions, no penalty. **Source**: https://www.randa.org/en/rog/the-rules-of-golf/rule-12

question: In stroke play, you hit your tee shot into the woods. You don't play a provisional. After searching for 2 minutes, your playing partner finds a ball. You identify it as yours but it's in an unplayable lie. You declare it unplayable and take lateral relief, dropping within two club-lengths. After you play your next shot, you discover the ball you found was actually not yours (the markings were similar). What is the ruling?
ground_truth: **Rule 6.3c & 18.1** - "In stroke play, the player gets the general penalty (two penalty strokes) and must correct the mistake" and "The stroke made with the wrong ball and any more strokes before the mistake is corrected do not count" **Ruling**: Your original ball was lost, so you must proceed under stroke-and-distance relief from the tee. **Penalties/Procedures**: Two-stroke penalty for playing wrong ball. All strokes with the wrong ball (unplayable relief and subsequent shot) don't count. Since your original ball was lost after the 3-minute search window, you must return to the tee and play your 4th shot (1 original + 1 lost ball penalty + 2 wrong ball penalty). **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules-hub/topics/out-of-bounds-lost-ball-provisional.html

question: In match play, your opponent's tee shot goes into a red penalty area. They drop under penalty of one stroke and hit their third shot onto the green. You then notice that when they dropped, they dropped on the wrong side of the penalty area (the side closer to the hole). You say nothing until after they two-putt for a 5. You then raise the issue. Is your opponent's score valid?
ground_truth: **Rule 20.1e** - "A ruling request must be made before either player makes a stroke to begin another hole" **Ruling**: Yes, your opponent's score is valid. You waited too long to raise the issue. **Penalties/Procedures**: In match play, you must raise rules violations before either player makes a stroke to begin the next hole, or the claim is invalid. Since you waited until after your opponent completed the hole, you forfeited your right to challenge the improper drop. The hole stands as played. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/articles/2019/03/golf-rules-dell-match-play.html

question: Your ball is on a steep slope in the general area. You carefully place your club behind the ball to address it, and the ball moves. Under the current Rules of Golf (2023), are you deemed to have caused the ball to move?
ground_truth: **Rule 9.4b** - "When the actions of a player cause their ball to move, the player is required to replace it before playing" **Ruling**: Yes, under the current rules, you are deemed to have caused the ball to move by addressing it, even if done carefully. **Penalties/Procedures**: One penalty stroke under Rule 9.4b. You must replace the ball to its original spot before making your next stroke. This applies even if you were careful and didn't intend to move it - the act of addressing caused the movement. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/dam/usga/pdf/2026/Additional%20Clarifications%20of%20the%202023%20Rules%20of%20Golf%20-%201%20January%202026%20-%20final%20(Dec%2023).pdf

question: Your ball lands in a flower bed adjacent to the clubhouse. The flower bed is not marked as GUR and there is no local rule about it. Your ball is playable. A committee member walking by tells you that flower beds are always treated as GUR. Is the committee member correct?
ground_truth: **Rule 16.1** and Committee Procedures - "Ground under repair is any part of the course the Committee defines to be ground under repair, whether by marking it or otherwise" **Ruling**: No, the committee member is incorrect. Flower beds are not automatically GUR unless specifically designated by the Committee. **Penalties/Procedures**: No automatic relief. Ground under repair must be specifically defined by the Committee, either by marking or in local rules. Just being a flower bed doesn't automatically make it GUR. You must play the ball as it lies unless the Committee has specifically declared this flower bed as GUR. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/custom-search-pages/rules/2019-golf-rules-and-interpretations/cp-rule-8.html

question: Your ball is unplayable in a penalty area. Can you declare it unplayable and take unplayable ball relief (back-on-the-line or two club-lengths) under Rule 19?
ground_truth: **Rule 19.1** - "You may take unplayable ball relief using one of the three options shown in Diagram 19.2, in each case adding one penalty stroke" and "Unplayable ball relief is allowed anywhere on the course, except in a penalty area" **Ruling**: No, you cannot declare a ball unplayable when it is in a penalty area. **Penalties/Procedures**: Must use penalty area relief options under Rule 17.1d (one penalty stroke). You cannot use Rule 19 unplayable ball relief when your ball is in a penalty area. Only penalty area relief procedures apply. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/rules-hub/topics/unplayable-ball.html

question: Preferred lies (lift, clean, and place) are in effect via local rule. Your ball is in the first cut of rough just off the fairway. Can you lift, clean, and place your ball under the preferred lies local rule?
ground_truth: **Model Local Rule E-3** - "If the preferred lies Local Rule (MLR E-3) is in place, relief is generally restricted to when your ball lies on the fairway" **Ruling**: No, you cannot use preferred lies relief in the first cut of rough. **Penalties/Procedures**: No penalty, but no relief available. Preferred lies local rules typically apply only to balls lying on the fairway, not in the rough. The local rule should specify the exact areas where preferred lies apply, but rough areas are generally excluded to prevent players from getting free relief from areas where the ball might otherwise be unplayable. **Source**: https://www.usga.org/content/usga/home-page/articles/2023/03/Spring_Golf_Rules.html
